facts.fact: Monotonic stack transforms O(n²) nested-loop problems into O(n) single-pass solutions by ensuring each element is pushed and popped exactly once
Prefix sum preprocessing enables O(1) range sum queries via formula Sum[l,r] = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1], achieving O(n+q) total complexity vs O(n×q) brute force
Segment trees achieve O(log n) for both range updates and range queries, while prefix sums are O(1) query but O(n) update cost on data changes
Fixed-size sliding window requires one complete array pass in O(n) time with O(1) space by maintaining running sum and slide-subtract-add operations
Variable-size sliding window uses two pointers (start/end) that expand and contract dynamically, maintaining O(n) time and O(k) space where k is distinct element count
Largest Rectangle in Histogram problem solved via monotonic stack in O(n) time by finding left/right boundaries for each bar in single pass
Daily Temperatures (LeetCode 739) uses decreasing monotonic stack to find next greater element indices; each element pushed/popped exactly once for O(n) complexity
Lazy propagation in segment trees defers updates to child nodes by storing update information in separate array, improving range update efficiency from O(n) to O(log n)
Monotonic stack tree using binary lifting can answer Range Min/Max queries (RMQ) in O(log n) per query by leveraging next-greater-element relationships
String problems combining sliding window + hash map track character frequencies in O(n) time O(d) space, where d is number of distinct characters
Next greater element trigger phrases include 'next greater', 'previous smaller', 'largest rectangle', 'maximum area', 'nearest larger/smaller' — all signal monotonic stack applicability
Fenwick trees (Binary Indexed Trees) are lighter on memory than segment trees for point updates to prefix sums, achieving O(log n) both operations
Common monotonic stack interview problems at FAANG include: LeetCode 496 (Next Greater I), 739 (Daily Temperatures), 84 (Largest Rectangle), 901 (Stock Span), 503 (Next Greater II)
Two-pointer initialization must match problem criteria; pointer movement depends on condition checks; off-by-one errors occur from incorrect stop conditions
For n=10⁶ elements and q=10⁶ queries: naive O(n×q) = 10¹² operations; prefix sum O(n+q) = 2×10⁶ operations (10,000× improvement)

examples.name: Daily Temperatures (LeetCode 739)
examples.description: Given array [73,74,75,71,69,72,76,73], output [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0] using decreasing monotonic stack. Input temperatures from real weather data. Each answer[i] is days until warmer temperature found.

examples.name: Largest Rectangle in Histogram (LeetCode 84)
examples.description: Given histogram heights [2,1,5,6,2,3], find max rectangle area=10 (width=2, height=5). Used in real image processing for connected component analysis.

examples.name: Next Greater Element I (LeetCode 496)
examples.description: Given nums1=[4,1,2], nums2=[1,3,4,2], output [-1,3,-1]. Real use: time-series data finding next signal spike greater than current value.

examples.name: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
examples.description: Input 'abcabcbb' outputs 3 (substring 'abc'). Variable sliding window + hash map tracks last-seen indices. Real use: validation scanning for duplicate tokens in logs.

examples.name: Stock Span (LeetCode 901)
examples.description: Given prices [100,80,60,70,60,75,85], spans [1,1,1,2,1,4,6]. Monotonic stack finds consecutive days prices ≤ current. Real use: financial span calculation for trading signals.

examples.name: Range Sum Query (1D Array)
examples.description: Array [3,1,6,5], query range [1,3]=12 answered in O(1) via prefix sums. Real use: database cumulative aggregations in O(1) after O(n) preprocessing.

analogies.analogy: A monotonic stack is like standing in a concert line where people are ordered by height. When a taller person arrives, everyone shorter than them steps aside (gets popped). The person directly in front (if any) is the 'next taller person' to their right.

A sliding window is like looking through a fixed-size glass pane moving across a landscape. You see only what fits in the pane. As you slide right, the left edge disappears, the right edge appears. For variable windows, the glass expands/contracts based on what you're looking for.

Prefix sums are like keeping a cumulative bank balance. Instead of recalculating total from transactions, you store running totals. To find balance in range [month 3 to month 7], subtract month-2 balance from month-7 balance.

Prefix sums are like a frozen snapshot (fast reads but expensive updates). Segment trees are like a live system (balanced read/write). Choose prefix if data is static; choose segment tree if data changes frequently.

Two pointers are like two runners on a track: left runner starts at start line, right runner at finish. They move based on rules (if left < target, move right; if left > target, move left). They meet when answer is found.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Monotonic stack requires popping ALL smaller elements when a larger one arrives
common_misconceptions.truth: Pop only while current > top AND while stack maintains order property. Stop and push current when condition breaks. This ensures O(n) not O(n²).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Sliding window always requires a hash map
common_misconceptions.truth: Fixed-size windows need only running sum/product. Hash maps are for variable windows tracking frequencies/uniqueness. Don't add unnecessary data structures.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prefix sums replace all array range queries
common_misconceptions.truth: Prefix sums only work for static arrays with no updates. If data changes, you need segment trees or Fenwick trees for O(log n) operations.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Two pointers require left pointer at start and right at end
common_misconceptions.truth: Pointer initialization depends entirely on problem. Left/right applies to sorted arrays; for string problems use start of window, end of window. Initialize based on problem structure.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Larger rectangles in histogram always use taller bars
common_misconceptions.truth: Largest rectangle area can use shorter bar with wide width. Monotonic stack finds optimal width bounds for each bar height by finding nearest smaller elements on both sides.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Segment trees are always better than prefix sums
common_misconceptions.truth: For query-only static data, prefix sums are simpler and faster (O(1) vs O(log n)). Use segment trees only when frequent updates occur.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Variable sliding window contracts after expanding
common_misconceptions.truth: Variable window expands until condition breaks, then contracts until condition is satisfied again. Both pointers move rightward overall, never 'reset'.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Monotonic stack only solves 'next greater' problems
common_misconceptions.truth: Applies to any problem finding: next-greater, next-smaller, previous-greater, previous-smaller, maximum-area, span calculations, and nested structure problems.
